title: Ensure that the --insecure-allow-any-token argument is not set

input_row.title: Ensure that the --insecure-allow-any-token argument is not set
input_row.description: Do not allow any insecure tokens
input_row.rationale: Accepting insecure tokens would allow any token without actually authenticating anything. User information is parsed from the token and connections are allowed.
input_row.audit: Run the following command on the master node: ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver Verify that the --insecure-allow-any-token argument does not exist.
input_row.remediation: Edit the /etc/kubernetes/apiserver file on the master node and remove the --insecure- allow-any-token argument from the KUBE_API_ARGS parameter. Based on your system, restart the kube-apiserver service. For example: systemctl restart kube-apiserver.service Impact: None Default Value: By default, insecure tokens are not allowed. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/   19 | P a g e
input_row.impact: None Default Value: By default, insecure tokens are not allowed.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The control requires verification that the kube-apiserver process is NOT running with the --insecure-allow-any-token argument.
- This flag is a process-level startup argument, not exposed via the Kubernetes API or kubectl.
- The audit procedure explicitly instructs to check the process arguments on the master node (ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver), which requires host-level access.
- Kubernetes APIs do not expose the full process command-line arguments for control plane components unless running in a managed environment that surfaces these details (which is not standard for self-managed clusters).
- Therefore, this check cannot be fully automated via Kubernetes APIs or kubectl and requires manual/host-level inspection.

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. SSH into each Kubernetes control plane (master) node.
2. Run the following command to list the kube-apiserver process and its arguments:
   ```
   ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver
   ```
3. Examine the output and verify that the --insecure-allow-any-token argument is NOT present in the command line used to start kube-apiserver.
4. If the argument is absent, the control is compliant. If present, remove it from the kube-apiserver startup configuration and restart the service to remediate.

**SOURCE:** CIS KUBERNETES 1.6 BENCHMARK V1.0.0 ARCHIVE
